aliases.name: Nikujin (肉人)
aliases.meaning: 'Meat-person'; related figure recorded in 1609 at Sunpu Castle

description.summary: A blob-like Japanese yokai resembling a soft, wrinkled mass of flesh, said to wander abandoned temples and graveyards. Depicted in classic Edo-period yokai scrolls including Toriyama Sekien's Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776).
description.full: The Nuppeppō (ぬっぺっぽう) is a yokai of Japanese folklore depicted as an amorphous, wrinkled blob of flesh — roughly humanoid in scale but lacking distinct features beyond rudimentary fingers, toes, and a face suggested by folds of skin. Tradition assigns it no eyes or ears, and it is generally treated as harmless, wandering deserted places: abandoned temples, graveyards, and empty village streets, especially at night and at year's end. Its name derives from nupperi (ぬっぺり), a derogatory term for women wearing heavy makeup, referencing the saggy face. The Nuppeppō is depicted in major Edo-period yokai scrolls including the Bakemono no e (c. 1700, held at Brigham Young University's L. Tom Perry Special Collections), Sawaki Suushi's Hyakkai Zukan (1737), and Toriyama Sekien's Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776). A related tradition — the nikujin ('meat-person') reported at Sunpu Castle in 1609 — is sometimes associated with the Nuppeppō; in some lore, eating its flesh confers immortality, echoing Chinese 'feng' or panacea-creature traditions.

etymology.origin: Japanese, from nupperi (ぬっぺり), a term referring to drooping or sagging — originally a derogatory description of women in heavy makeup
etymology.first_use: Edo period; depicted by name in 18th-century yokai scrolls

related_phenomena.name: Noppera-bō
related_phenomena.description: Faceless yokai sometimes confused with or treated as a precursor to the Nuppeppō

related_phenomena.name: Hitotsume-kozō
related_phenomena.description: Other Edo-period featureless yokai compiled by Toriyama Sekien

features.description: Roughly human-sized mass of soft, wrinkled flesh
Tradition (e.g. Sekien) holds it has neither eye nor ear
Rudimentary digits visible within the folds of skin
Suggested by folds of skin rather than fully formed
Said to smell strongly of rotting flesh

physical.morphology: A roughly human-scale, undifferentiated mass of soft, sagging flesh.

abilities.description: Drifts aimlessly through abandoned temples, graveyards, and empty streets
Some accounts hold that consuming the Nuppeppō's flesh grants long life or immortality, paralleling Chinese 'feng' or panacea traditions

patterns: Most active at night and at year's end

range.description: Japan; an Edo-period yokai documented in classical Japanese yokai scrolls.

hotspots.name: Sunpu Castle

location.description: Sunpu Castle (modern Shizuoka), residence of Tokugawa Ieyasu

hotspots.notes: Site of the 1609 nikujin ('meat-person') incident sometimes associated with Nuppeppō tradition

first_recorded.source: Bakemono no e scroll, c. 1700; subsequent Edo-period yokai compilations

origins.cultural_roots: Edo-period Japanese yokai literature, drawing on a wider tradition of grotesque and harmless wandering spirits.
origins.earliest_accounts: Bakemono no e (c. 1700), Sawaki Suushi's Hyakkai Zukan (1737), and Toriyama Sekien's Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776).
origins.folklore_connections: Connects to broader Edo-period yokai tradition (Sekien's compilations) and to Chinese 'feng' / panacea-creature traditions invoked in the immortality-flesh motif.

timeline.event: A nikujin ('meat-person') is reported at Sunpu Castle (Tokugawa Ieyasu's residence); later commentators connect this incident to Chinese 'feng' panacea-creature lore and to Nuppeppō tradition
Bakemono no e scroll (c. 1700) depicts the Nuppeppō; held at Brigham Young University's L. Tom Perry Special Collections
timeline.significance: Earliest visual attestation of the Nuppeppō by name
timeline.source: Bakemono no e (BYU L. Tom Perry Special Collections)

timeline.event: Sawaki Suushi includes the Nuppeppō in the Hyakkai Zukan (1737)
Toriyama Sekien depicts the Nuppeppō in the Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776)
timeline.significance: Most influential and widely-reproduced visual depiction

history.modern_era: The Nuppeppō appears regularly in modern yokai illustration, manga (e.g. Shigeru Mizuki's GeGeGe no Kitarō), anime, and games, often as a grotesque-but-harmless background figure.

documentary.name: Bakemono no e (c. 1700)
documentary.description: Edo-period yokai scroll depicting the Nuppeppō, held at Brigham Young University's L. Tom Perry Special Collections.
documentary.analysis: Earliest known illustrated attestation of the Nuppeppō by name.
documentary.source: L. Tom Perry Special Collections, BYU

documentary.name: Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776)
documentary.description: Toriyama Sekien's standard yokai compilation including the Nuppeppō.

paranormal.name: Wandering flesh-spirit
paranormal.description: Within Edo-period yokai tradition the Nuppeppō is a real but harmless spirit-being haunting deserted places.
evidence_cited: Recurrent depiction in Edo yokai scrolls

skeptical.name: Edo-period bestiary figure
skeptical.description: The Nuppeppō functions primarily as a Sekien-canonical bestiary figure, codified by 18th-century artists and inheriting motifs from earlier flesh-creature traditions (e.g. the 1609 nikujin).
evidence_cited: Documented codification through Bakemono no e, Hyakkai Zukan, and Gazu Hyakki Yagyō

stories: Wandering Nuppeppō encountered in abandoned temples
folklore.beliefs: Treated as a grotesque but generally harmless yokai; in rare traditions its flesh is said to confer immortality.

film_tv: GeGeGe no Kitarō (Shigeru Mizuki, 1960s onward) — the Nuppeppō appears as a recurring yokai
other: Standard appearances in modern yokai illustrated encyclopaedias

researchers.name: Toriyama Sekien
researchers.contribution: Standardised the visual depiction of the Nuppeppō in the Gazu Hyakki Yagyō (1776)

researchers.name: Sawaki Suushi
researchers.contribution: Earlier yokai-scroll Hyakkai Zukan (1737) including the Nuppeppō

researchers.name: Katsumi Tada
researchers.contribution: Modern scholarly analysis of yokai including the Nuppeppō / noppera-bō distinction

researchers.name: Morihiko Fujisawa
researchers.contribution: Established the Nuppeppō's traditional association with abandoned temples

research.ongoing_studies: Active topic in Japanese yokai studies, including at the International Research Center for Japanese Studies (Nichibunken).
